questions.question: List and briefly describe the four major categories of biomedical waste under WHO classification.
keyPoints: Infectious waste - contains pathogens (cultures, swabs, contaminated materials)
Pathological waste - human/animal tissues, organs, body parts
Sharps - items causing puncture/cuts (needles, blades, broken glass)
Pharmaceutical waste - expired drugs, vaccines, cytotoxic medicines

questions.question: Explain the difference between infectious waste and pathological waste with examples.
keyPoints: Infectious waste: materials contaminated with pathogens in sufficient concentration to cause disease - cultures, bandages, gloves, excreta from isolation wards
Pathological waste: recognizable human/animal tissues, organs, body parts - surgical waste, amputated limbs, placenta, animal carcasses
Key difference: pathological is anatomical/histological, infectious is microbiological contamination

questions.question: Enumerate at least five major sources of biomedical waste generation.
keyPoints: Hospitals (multi-specialty, nursing homes, primary health centres)
Diagnostic laboratories (pathology, microbiology, radiology)
Research institutions (biological research, animal houses)
Blood banks and transfusion centres
Pharmaceutical industries and manufacturing units

questions.question: What is cytotoxic waste? Why does it require special handling and disposal?
keyPoints: Cytotoxic waste contains genotoxic substances - drugs used in chemotherapy that can cause genetic mutations

Special handling required because: (1) These substances are carcinogenic (cancer-causing) (2) They can cause birth defects (teratogenic) (3) They affect DNA of healthy cells (4) Requires specialized incineration at high temperatures (5) Personnel must have special training and PPE

questions.question: Differentiate between chemical waste and pharmaceutical waste in biomedical waste management context.
keyPoints: Chemical waste: inorganic substances - disinfectants (phenol, chlorine), solvents, heavy metals (mercury), formaldehyde, laboratory reagents, photographic chemicals
Pharmaceutical waste: medicinal products - expired antibiotics, unused tablets/syrups, vaccines, discarded IV fluids
Both require different disposal methods: chemical often needs incineration or chemical treatment, pharmaceutical may need encapsulation or supervised landfill

questions.question: According to Biomedical Waste Management Rules 2016 in India, list the 10 categories of BMW.
keyPoints: 1) Human Anatomical Waste 2) Animal Anatomical Waste 3) Soiled Waste 4) Sharps 5) Discarded Glassware 6) Microbiology & Biotechnology Waste 7) Cytotoxic Drugs 8) Chemical Waste 9) Pharmaceutical Waste 10) Radioactive Waste

questions.question: Explain the various types of biomedical waste with their characteristics, hazards, and examples from healthcare facilities.
keyPoints: 1) Infectious: pathogens in sufficient concentration - cultures, swabs, contaminated materials; hazard: disease transmission
2) Pathological: recognizable tissues, organs - surgical waste, amputated limbs, placenta; hazard: infection, emotional impact
3) Sharps: puncture-causing items - needles, blades, broken glass; hazard: injury + blood-borne infections
4) Pharmaceutical: expired/unused drugs - antibiotics, vaccines, cytotoxic drugs; hazard: toxicity, antibiotic resistance
5) Cytotoxic: genotoxic substances - chemotherapy drugs; hazard: cancer, mutations, birth defects
6) Chemical: toxic substances - mercury, formaldehyde, disinfectants; hazard: poisoning, environmental damage
7) Radioactive: isotopes - Iodine-131, Cobalt-60; hazard: radiation sickness, cancer
8) General: non-hazardous - paper, plastics, food; minimal hazard if properly managed

questions.question: Discuss the major sources of biomedical waste and explain why proper identification of sources is crucial for effective BMW management.
keyPoints: Sources: (A) Hospitals - generates largest volume, all categories present (B) Diagnostic labs - microbiology cultures, chemicals (C) Research institutions - animal houses, biological research (D) Blood banks - bags, reagents (E) Nursing homes/clinics - smaller but significant (F) Funeral homes - pathological waste (G) Pharmaceutical industries

Importance of source identification: (1) Determines waste category and handling (2) Helps estimate quantity and type (3) Enables color-coded segregation at source (4) Training requirements for staff (5) Choosing appropriate treatment technology (6) Legal compliance under BMW Rules 2016 (7) Cost estimation for waste management
